text: The surgeon closes the fascial defect along the long axis using a sero-barred slowly absorbable suture, running it continuously along the length of the defect. The suture is tightened at the opposite end, then run back in the opposite direction to create overlapping, and tightened again.

In the second case of recurrent incisional hernia, the surgeon performs lysis of adhesions and reduces the hernia contents. The fascial defect is closed along the long axis using a zero-barred, slowly absorbable suture, with abdominal pressure reduced to six to eight millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon tightens the suture along the length of the large fascial defect. A second suture is then applied in the opposite direction, from caudal to cranial, to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon secures the mesh circumferentially using a tool-barred slowly absorbable suture, ensuring the mesh is firmly attached to the surrounding tissue.
